title: Defining transformations
description: Refine definitions of rigid transformations. Given a description of the effect of a transformation, determine which rigid transformation it is.

assessment_items.question_content:
Anju tried to define a reflection.

- For any point $R$ on the line of reflection $\ell$ , the image $R'$ is at the same point as $R$.
- For any point $P$ **not** on the line of reflection $\ell$, the image $P'$ is on the other side of $\ell$ such that $\overline{PP'}$ is perpendicular to $\ell$.

**What mistake did Anju make in her definition of a reflection?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: From Anju's description, it is possible to draw more than one point $P'$ for a given point $P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Points $Q$ and $R$ are both on the other side of $\ell$. Also, $\overline{PQ}$ and $\overline{PR}$ are both perpendicular to $\ell$.

What additional information did we need to know that whether $P'$ is $Q$ or $R$?
Points $P$ and $P'$ must be the same distance from the line of reflection.

choices.content: $\overline{PP'}$ must be parallel to the line of reflection, not perpendicular.
Points $P$ and $P'$ must be the same distance from the line of reflection.
Anju needed to include the direction of the reflection.
Anju did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Shawn tried to define a reflection.

- For any point $R$ on the line of reflection $\ell$ , the image $R'$ is at the same point as $R$.
- For any point $P$ **not** on the line of reflection $\ell$, the image $P'$ is on the other side of $\ell$ such that $P$ and $P'$ are the same distance from $\ell$.

**What mistake did Shawn make in his definition of a reflection?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: From Shawn's description, it is possible to draw more than one point $P'$ for a given point $P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Points $Q$ and $R$ are both on the other side of $\ell$, and they are both the same distance from $\ell$ as $P$.

What additional information did we need to know that whether $P'$ is $Q$ or $R$?
$PP'$ must be perpendicular to the line of reflection.

choices.content: $PP'$ must be perpendicular to the line of reflection.
There must exist a point $L$ on the line of reflection such that angle $PLP'$ is a right angle.
All points of the image must be on the same side of the line of reflection.
Shawn did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Heng tried to define a reflection across line $r$.

- Any point $N$ on line $r$ maps to itself.
- Any point $M$ not on the line of reflection maps to a point $M'$ such that the midpoint $L$ of $\overline{MM'}$ is on line $r$.

**Which counterexample shows that Heng's definition does not fully define a reflection?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **counterexample** is an exception that proves a proposed rule or definition wrong. 

So our counterexample could be either of the following:

- An example of a reflection that does not match Heng's definition
- An example of a figure that matches Heng's definition, but is not a reflection

It only takes one counterexample to show that the proposed definition has a mistake or is incomplete.

This matches Heng's definition, but is not a reflection, because $MM'$ is not perpendicular to line $r$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6cd36512eb2ed8a6198e28544dd41e6517a8781d)

So it is a counterexample disproving Heng's definition.

choices.content:
![Points L and N are on line r. Point N is the same point for N prime. Line R is perpendicular to segment M M prime. Point L is the midpoint of line segment M M prime. Line segment M L and line segment L M prime are congruent.
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c8dc1ff4ecfd5f6ce502e67c84f8f4758e9ee5b5)

![Points L and N are on line r. Point N is the same point for N prime. Line segment M L and line segment L M prime are congruent make a right angle above line r.  
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6e78462001d4ad793d2ce27f8d9266238f4f4866)

![Points L and N are on line r. Point N is the same point for N prime. The point L is the center of a circle. Line segment M L and line segment L M prime are congruent and together make a diameter M M prime for the circle. The line segment M M prime is not perpendicular to line r.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6cd36512eb2ed8a6198e28544dd41e6517a8781d)
Heng fully defined a reflection.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sasha tried to define a reflection across line $\ell$.

- Any point $Q$ on line of reflection $\ell$ maps to itself.
- Any point $P$ not on $\ell$ maps to a point $P'$ such that $P$ and $P'$ are the same distance from $\ell$.

**Which counterexample shows that Sasha's definition does not fully define a reflection?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **counterexample** is an exception that proves a proposed rule or definition wrong. 

So our counterexample could be either of the following:

- An example of a reflection that does not match Sasha's definition
- An example of a figure that matches Sasha's definition, but is not a reflection

It only takes one counterexample to show that the proposed definition has a mistake or is incomplete.

This matches Sasha's definition, but is not a reflection, because $PP'$ is not perpendicular to $\ell$, and because both $P$ and $P'$ are on the same side of $\ell$.


[[☃ image 1]]

So it is a counterexample disproving Sasha's definition.

choices.content: ![An unlabeled point and point Q are on line l. Point P is the same point for P prime. Line l is perpendicular to segment P P prime. The unlabeled point is the midpoint of line segment P P prime. Line segment P and unlabeled point and line segment P prime and unlabeled point P prime are congruent.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b221234b32929060cb4e3a46117fd2dd4b48814d)

![An unlabeled point and point Q are on line l. Point Q is the same point for Q prime. Line l is perpendicular to segment P on its upper is upper side. Next to it is segment P prime also on the same upper side of line l. Both segments connect to unknown points on line l.  Line segment P and unlabeled point and line segment P prime and unlabeled point P prime are congruent.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9dd35974a23e4e74b6359d974b4a98e4873b0647)

![An unlabeled point and point Q are on line l. Point P is the same point for P prime. Line l is perpendicular to segment P P prime. The unlabeled point is not the midpoint of line segment P P prime. Line segment P and unlabeled point and line segment P prime and unlabeled point P prime are not congruent.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/72e50ac559c44faa03982fe79caaa2db7989aedf)
Sasha fully defined a reflection.

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain mapping in the $xy$-plane has the following properties:

- Every point $A$ on line $\ell$ maps to itself.
- Every point $P$ that isn't on $\ell$ maps to point $P'$ such that $\ell$ is the perpendicular bisector of $PP'$

**Which transformation does the mapping define?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's draw a line $\ell$ and a point $A$ on that line.


[[☃ image 1]]

Then we can draw several random points, perform the transformation, and see what happens!

[[☃ image 1]]

Each point maps to the other side of $\ell$ and remains the same distance from $\ell$. Also, the segments between each pre-image and image are perpendicular to $\ell$. Therefore, this transformation is a reflection.
The mapping defines a reflection.

choices.content: A translation
A reflection
A rotation

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain mapping in the $xy$-plane has the following properties:

- Every point $A$ on line $\ell$ maps to itself.
- Every point $P$ that isn't on $\ell$ maps to a point $P'$ such that $\overline{PP'}$ is the diameter of a circle centered on $\ell$, and $\overline{PP'}$ is perpendicular to $\ell$.

**Which transformation does the mapping define?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's draw a line $\ell$ and a point $A$ on that line.


[[☃ image 1]]

Then we can draw a few random points, perform the transformation, and see what happens!

[[☃ image 1]]

Each point maps to the other side of $\ell$ and remains the same distance from $\ell$. Also, the segments between each pre-image and image are perpendicular to $\ell$. Therefore, this transformation is a reflection.
The mapping defines a reflection.

choices.content: A translation
A reflection
A rotation

assessment_items.question_content:
Jack tried to define a rotation.

- For the center point $C$, the image $C'$ is at the same point as $C$.
- For a point $P$ that is not at $C$, a rotation of $\theta$ about point $C$ creates point $P'$, such that $CP=CP'$ and $m\angle PCP'=\theta$.

**What mistake did Jack make in his definition of a rotation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: From Jack's description, it is possible to draw more than one point $P'$ for a given point $P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

$CP=CQ=CR$ and $m\angle PCQ=m\angle PCR=\theta$.

What additional information did we need to know that whether $P'$ is $Q$ or $R$?
Jack needed to include the direction of the rotation.

choices.content: Jack needed to include the direction of the rotation.
$m\angle CPP'$ must equal $\theta$.
Point $C$ must be the midpoint of $PP'$.
Jack did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Layla tried to define a rotation.

- For the center point $C$, the image $C'$ is at the same point as $C$.
- For a point $P$ that is not at $C$, a counterclockwise rotation of $\theta$ about point $C$ creates point $P'$, such that $m\angle PCP'=\theta$.

**What mistake did Layla make in her definition of a rotation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: From Layla's description, it is possible to draw more than one point $P'$ for a given point $P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The rotation shown here is counterclockwise with $m\angle PCQ=m\angle PCR=\theta$.

What additional information did we need to know that whether $P'$ is $Q$ or $R$?
The lengths of $\overline{CP}$ and $\overline{CP'}$ must be equal.

choices.content: Layla needed to include the distance between each point $P$ and its image $P'$.
$CP$ must be perpendicular to $CP'$.
The lengths of $\overline{CP}$ and $\overline{CP'}$ must be equal.
Layla did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Luka tried to define a rotation about point $C$.

- For the center point $C$, the image $C'$ is at the same point as $C$.
- The distances between each pre-image and its image are all equal.
- For a point $P$ that is not at $C$, a counterclockwise rotation of $\theta$ about point $C$ creates point $P'$, such that $CP=CP'$ and $m\angle PCP'=\theta$.

**Which counterexample shows that Luka's definition does not define a rotation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **counterexample** is an exception that proves a proposed rule or definition wrong. 

So our counterexample could be either of the following:

- An example of a rotation that does not match Luka's definition
- An example of a figure that matches Luka's definition, but is not a rotation

It only takes one counterexample to show that the proposed definition has a mistake or is incomplete.

This is a rotation, but it doesn't match Luka's definition since the distance between $P$ and $P'$ is larger than the distance between $R$ and $R'$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/917b8da00dcbe1d32602e39a0eb5f76cb89f48f0)

So it is a counterexample disproving Luka's definition.

choices.content: ![Four line segments C R, C R prime, C P, and C P prime centered around point C. Line segment C R prime is a rotation of line C R by theta. Line segment C P prime is a rotation of line segments C P by a rotation of theta. Line segment C P is longer than line segment C R. There are curved dashed arcs highlighting that the difference in distance between P and P prime is larger than the distance between R and R prime.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/917b8da00dcbe1d32602e39a0eb5f76cb89f48f0)

![Four line segments C R, C R prime, C P, and C P prime centered around point C. The angle between R C and C R prime is theta, but line segment C R prime is shorter than line segment C R. The angle between P C and C P prime is theta, but line segment C P prime is longer than line segment C P.  Line segment C R and line segment C P are congruent, which is highlighted by a curved dashed arc representing a circle.
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9b0a559733f4e9750f259cd61404e53d0f7e2795)

![Four line segments C R, C R prime, C P, and C P prime centered around point C. Line segment C R prime is a rotation of line segment C R by theta. Line segment C P prime is a rotation of line segment C P by a rotation of theta. Line segment C P is congruent to line segment C R. There is a curved dashed arc that goes through every point except C.
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8f3e601a66594c6db628a3ddc83d1b90f4acfa62)
Luka correctly defined a rotation.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rosalyn tried to define a rotation about point $C$.

- For the center point $C$, the image $C'$ is at the same point as $C$.
- For a point $P$ that is not at $C$, a counterclockwise rotation of $\theta$ about point $C$ creates point $P'$, such that
 - $m\angle PCP'=\theta$
 - Point $C$ is the midpoint of $PP'$

**Which counterexample shows that Rosalyn's definition does not define a rotation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **counterexample** is an exception that proves a proposed rule or definition wrong. 

So our counterexample could be either of the following:

- An example of a rotation that does not match Rosalyn's definition
- An example of a figure that matches Rosalyn's definition, but is not a rotation

It only takes one counterexample to show that the proposed definition has a mistake or is incomplete.

This is a rotation, but it doesn't match Rosalyn's definition since $C$ is not the midpoint of $PP'$.


[[☃ image 1]]

So it is a counterexample disproving Rosalyn's definition.

choices.content: ![Two line segments C P and C P prime centered around point C. Line segment C P prime is a rotation of line C P by one hundred eighty degrees. Line segment C P has a congruent symbol with Line segment C P prime  There is a curved dashed arc highlighting rotation.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41cb21e88274da1674b851e4000d0e171816688f)

![Two line segments C P and C P prime centered around point C. Line segment C P prime is a rotation of line C P by theta. Line segment C P has a congruent symbol with Line segment C P prime  There is a curved dashed arc highlighting rotation.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ac2d14ae645dfe2fa0b8b8c7fcd140810f31724c)

![Two line segments C P and C P prime centered around point C. Line segments C P prime and C P are both horizontal. Line segment C P is longer than Line segment C P prime  There is a curved dashed arc highlighting difference between the lengths of segments C P and C P prime.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/13749651975875844e4eb1ebe915db109476c74f)
Rosalyn correctly defined a rotation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain mapping in the $xy$-plane has the following properties:

- For the point $A$, the image $A'$ is at the same point as $A$.
- A point $P$ that is not at $A$ maps counterclockwise to point $P'$ such that $m\angle PAP'=\theta$.
- The lengths of $\overline{AP}$ and $\overline{AP'}$ are equal.

**Which transformation does the mapping define?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's pick a point $A$ that will map to itself. 


[[☃ image 1]]

Then we can draw several random points, perform the transformation, and see what happens!

[[☃ image 1]]

Each point gets rotated by the same angle in the same direction about the center point $A$. Also, each pre-image is the same distance from $A$ as its image. Therefore, this transformation is a rotation.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The mapping defines a rotation.

choices.content: A translation
A reflection
A rotation

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain mapping in the $xy$-plane has the following properties:

- For the point $A$, the image $A'$ is at the same point as $A$.
- A point $P$ that is not at $A$ maps to a point $P'$ clockwise along the circumference of a circle centered at $A$ and with a radius of $AP$.
- $m\angle PAP' = \theta$.

**Which transformation does the mapping define?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's pick a point $A$ that will map to itself. 


[[☃ image 1]]

Then we can draw a few random points, perform the transformation, and see what happens!

[[☃ image 1]]

Each point gets rotated by the same angle in the same direction about the center point $A$. Also, each pre-image is the same distance from $A$ as its image. Therefore, this transformation is a rotation.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The mapping defines a rotation.

choices.content: A translation
A reflection
A rotation

assessment_items.question_content:
Tony tried to define a translation.

- Point $A$ maps to point $A'$.
- Every point $P$ maps to point $P'$ such that $\overrightarrow{PP'}$ is parallel to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$ and points in the same direction as $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.

**What mistake did Tony make in his definition of a translation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: From Tony's description, it is possible to draw more than one point $P'$ for a given point $P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Both $\overrightarrow{PQ'}$ and $\overrightarrow{PR}$ are parallel to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$ and point in the same direction as $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.

What additional information did we need to know that whether $P'$ is $Q$ or $R$?
$\overline{PP'}$ must have the same length as $\overline{AA'}$.

choices.content: Tony needed to mention that the center of translation maps to itself.
$\overline{PP'}$ must have the same length as $\overline{AA'}$.
$\overrightarrow{PP'}$ must be perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.
Tony did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tobin tried to define a translation.

- Point $A$ maps to point $A'$.
- Every point $P$ maps to point $P'$ such that $\overline{PP'}$ has the same length as $\overline{AA'}$.

**What mistake did Tobin make in her definition of a translation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: From Tobin's description, it is possible to draw more than one point $P'$ for a given point $P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Both $\overline{PR}$ and $\overline{PQ}$ have the same length as $\overline{AA'}$.

What additional information did we need to know that whether $P'$ is $Q$ or $R$?
$\overrightarrow{PP'}$ must be parallel to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$ and point in the same direction as $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.

choices.content: Tobin needed to define an angle $\theta$ formed by $\overrightarrow{AA'}$ and $\overrightarrow{PP'}$.
Tobin needed to mention which points map to themselves.
$\overrightarrow{PP'}$ must be parallel to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$ and point in the same direction as $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.
Tobin did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Anna tried to define a translation.

- Point $A$ maps to point $A'$.
- Every point $P$ maps to point $P'$ such that $\overrightarrow{PP'}$ is parallel to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.
- $\overline{PP'}$ has the same length as $\overline{AA'}$.

**Which counterexample shows that Anna's definition does not fully define a translation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **counterexample** is an exception that proves a proposed rule or definition wrong. 

So our counterexample could be either of the following:

- An example of a translation that does not match Anna's definition
- An example of a figure that matches Anna's definition, but is not a translation

It only takes one counterexample to show that the proposed definition has a mistake or is incomplete.

This matches Anna's definition, but is not a translation, because not every point moved in the same direction.


[[☃ image 1]]

So it is a counterexample disproving Anna's definition.

choices.content:
![Ray A A prime points left to right. Below is ray P P prime that points left to right. The rays are equal in length.
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dfe9e14648dac205e1072029cd012fb96f7178d1)

![Ray A A prime points left to right. Below is ray P P prime that points left to right. Ray P P prime is twice the size of ray A A prime.
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/654c976a805b383988f9b10c65a7bd54700228bb)

![Ray A A prime points left to right. Below is ray P P prime that points right to left. The rays are equal in length.
](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/de6f1f63b1de783238cbe39a45ef34cd432e9647)
Anna fully defined a translation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain mapping in the $xy$-plane has the following properties:

- Point $A$ maps to point $A'$.
- Every point $P$ maps to point $P'$ such that:
 - $\overline{PP'}$ has a length of $AA'$
 - $\overrightarrow{PP'}$ is parallel to $\overrightarrow{AA'}$ and points in the same direction as $\overrightarrow{AA'}$.

**Which transformation does the mapping define?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's pick a directed line segment $AA'$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Then we can draw several random points, perform the transformation, and see what happens!

[[☃ image 1]]

Each point moves the same distance in the same direction. Therefore, this transformation is a translation.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The mapping defines a translation.

choices.content: A translation
A reflection
A rotation

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain mapping in the $xy$-plane has the following properties:

- Every point moves $m$ units horizontally and $n$ units vertically.
- If $m$ is positive, the point moves to the right. If $m$ is negative, the point moves to the left.
- If $n$ is positive, the point moves up. If $n$ is negative, the point moves down.

**Which transformation does the mapping define?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's draw a few random points, perform the transformation, and see what happens!


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

Each point moves the same distance in the same direction. Therefore, this transformation is a translation.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The mapping defines a translation.

choices.content: A translation
A reflection
A rotation
